lessons.title: Page Builder Fundamentals: Sections, Blocks, and Global Styles
lessons.title_es: Fundamentos del Constructor de Páginas: Secciones, Bloques y Estilos Globales
lessons.description: Master the three-layer architecture of NetWeb CMS pages and set up global styles that propagate changes site-wide instantly.
lessons.description_es: Domina la arquitectura de tres capas de las páginas de NetWeb CMS y configura estilos globales que propagan cambios en todo el sitio al instante.
lessons.content:
The Three-Layer Page Model

Every page in NetWeb CMS is built from three layers: Sections (full-width containers with their own background), Columns (horizontal divisions within a section), and Blocks (individual content units — text, image, video, form, button). Understanding this hierarchy prevents the most common layout mistakes.

A typical homepage structure: Hero section (1 column, full-bleed image background) → Features section (3-column grid, white background) → Testimonials section (1 column, dark background) → CTA section (1 column, brand color background). Each section is independently configurable for spacing, background, visibility, and animation.

Global Styles: Set Once, Win Everywhere

Global Styles is the most powerful feature new CMS users ignore. Set it up before building a single page. Navigate to CMS → Design → Global Styles.

  • Typography: Set H1–H6 sizes, weights, line-heights, and the body font. Any heading block on any page inherits these unless overridden. For NetWebMedia: H1 = Poppins 700 48px, Body = Inter 400 16px 1.6 line-height.
  • Colors: Define your brand palette as named tokens: Primary = #010F3B, Accent = #FF671F, Surface = #1E293B, Text = #CBD5E1. Use token names in blocks, not hex codes — when you change a token, every instance updates.
  • Buttons: Define Primary, Secondary, and Ghost button styles once. Every button block defaults to these unless explicitly overridden.
  • Spacing scale: Define your spacing tokens (xs=8px, sm=16px, md=32px, lg=64px, xl=96px). Use these in section padding to keep vertical rhythm consistent.

Block Efficiency: Templates vs. Custom

NetWeb CMS ships with 40+ block templates. Before building a custom block, check the library. The time cost of custom blocks is real: each custom block needs testing across 3 breakpoints (desktop, tablet, mobile) and must be maintained when the design system updates.

When you must go custom: use the Code Block for HTML/CSS/JS, or the Template Block system to create reusable blocks that appear in the library for your whole team. Template Blocks are the right answer for any block pattern you'll use on more than 2 pages.

lessons.title: Blog System: SEO-Ready Posts, Structured Data, and Collections
lessons.title_es: Sistema de Blog: Posts Optimizados para SEO, Datos Estructurados y Colecciones
lessons.description: Publish blog posts with built-in schema markup, configure collections for content hubs, and set up the RSS feed and sitemap integration.
lessons.description_es: Publica posts con marcado de esquema integrado, configura colecciones para hubs de contenido y configura la integración de feed RSS y sitemap.
lessons.content:
Post Architecture for SEO

Every blog post in NetWeb CMS has two layers: the Content Layer (what readers see) and the SEO Layer (what search engines read). Most marketers fill in the content layer and ignore the SEO layer. That's why their posts don't rank.

The SEO Layer fields you must fill on every post:

  • Title tag: 50–60 characters. Primary keyword near the front. Not the same as your H1 (your H1 can be longer and more engaging).
  • Meta description: 120–155 characters. Include primary keyword and a clear benefit statement. This is your search result ad copy.
  • Canonical URL: Always set. Prevents duplicate content issues if the post appears in multiple collections.
  • Schema type: Select Article for standard posts, FAQPage for Q&A format, HowTo for step-by-step guides. NetWeb CMS generates the JSON-LD automatically based on your selection and post content.
  • Focus keyword: Enter the primary keyword. CMS calculates keyword density, checks it's in the H1, first paragraph, at least one H2, and the meta description. Green = ready to publish.

Collections: Powering Your Content Hubs

Collections are groups of posts that share a taxonomy. Think: an Industry Hub page that dynamically pulls all posts tagged with a specific industry. Collections update automatically — add a new post with the right tag and it appears on the hub page without touching the hub page itself.

Create a collection under Blog → Collections → New Collection. Define: name, filter rules (tags, categories, authors, date ranges), sort order, and the number of posts to display. Then drop a Blog Grid Block on any page and connect it to the collection. That page now auto-updates with every new qualifying post.

Publishing Checklist (Pre-Publish)

  • Title tag ≤ 60 characters with primary keyword
  • Meta description 120–155 characters
  • At least one internal link to a related post or service page
  • At least one external link to a credible source
  • Featured image set (1200×630px minimum, ALT text written)
  • Schema type selected
  • Published date set (affects Article schema datePublished)
  • Author profile linked (affects E-E-A-T signals)

lessons.title: Memberships and LMS: Gating Content and Tracking Learner Progress
lessons.title_es: Membresías y LMS: Acceso Restringido a Contenido y Seguimiento del Progreso del Alumno
lessons.description: Configure membership tiers, gate pages and courses behind paywalls, and track learner completion with the built-in progress dashboard.
lessons.description_es: Configura niveles de membresía, restringe acceso a páginas y cursos, y rastrea la finalización del aprendiz con el panel de progreso integrado.
lessons.content:
Membership Tiers Architecture

NetWeb CMS memberships use a Role → Tier → Content model. Roles are the base permission level (Guest, Member, Pro, Admin). Tiers are named groupings you create (Free, Starter, Pro, Agency). Content visibility is controlled at the page level, block level, or both.

Set up tiers under CMS → Memberships → Tiers. For each tier define: name, price (monthly/annual), feature list (used on the pricing display block), and which roles are included. A Pro tier might include Member and Pro roles — users at this tier see all Member content plus Pro-only content.

Gating Content: Page-Level vs. Block-Level

Page-level gating: The entire page requires login + specific tier. Set under each page's Settings → Access. Unauthenticated users see a configurable redirect (login page, upgrade page, or a teaser page). Use page-level gating for course modules, member resources, and private dashboards.

Block-level gating: Specific sections of a page are hidden or replaced based on tier. A pricing page might show full feature details to Pro members but show a blurred/teaser version to free members with an upgrade CTA overlaid. Set per-block under Block Settings → Visibility → Membership Rules.

LMS Course Structure

The LMS module structures learning content as: Course → Module → Lesson. Each lesson has a type (Video, Text, Quiz, Assignment) and a completion trigger (watched X%, clicked complete, passed quiz with score ≥ Y%).

  • Progress tracking: The learner dashboard shows completion percentage per module and course. Instructors see a roster view with individual learner progress and time-on-page data.
  • Drip content: Lock lessons until prerequisites are complete, or unlock on a schedule (Lesson 2 unlocks 3 days after enrollment). Configure per-lesson under Lesson Settings → Unlock Rules.
  • Completion certificates: Auto-generate a PDF certificate when a learner completes 100% of a course. Upload your certificate template as an SVG with merge field placeholders: {{learner_name}}, {{course_name}}, {{completion_date}}.
  • Quiz engine: Multiple choice, true/false, and short answer. Set pass threshold, number of attempts allowed, and whether wrong answers reveal the correct answer immediately or after the attempt is exhausted.

lessons.title: Launch Workflow: From Draft to Live in Under 2 Hours
lessons.title_es: Flujo de Lanzamiento: De Borrador a Publicado en Menos de 2 Horas
lessons.description: The complete go-live checklist — DNS, redirects, speed optimizations, and the post-launch monitoring setup every new site needs.
lessons.description_es: La lista completa para salir en vivo — DNS, redirecciones, optimizaciones de velocidad y la configuración de monitoreo post-lanzamiento que todo nuevo sitio necesita.
lessons.content:
The 5-Phase Launch Workflow

Launching a site without a checklist is how you end up with broken redirects, missing canonical tags, and a Google index of your staging domain. This 5-phase workflow covers every step from final QA to post-launch monitoring.

Phase 1 — Content QA (45 min)

  • Read every page on the site. Not skim — read. Catch grammar, broken links, placeholder text.
  • Test all forms: submit each one with a real email address and confirm the notification arrives and the CRM contact is created.
  • Test all CTAs: every button goes to the right destination.
  • Mobile check: view every page on a real phone (not just browser dev tools). Check touch targets, font sizes, image aspect ratios.

Phase 2 — SEO Pre-Launch (20 min)

  • Confirm robots.txt allows all pages you want indexed. Staging should have been set to noindex — switch to index before launch.
  • Verify sitemap.xml is generated and includes all pages.
  • Check canonical tags on every page — should all point to the production domain, not staging.
  • Confirm 301 redirects for any old URLs that are changing (e.g., /old-about → /about). Map them in CMS → Redirects.

Phase 3 — Performance (15 min)

  • Run a PageSpeed Insights test on the homepage and one long-form page. Target: LCP under 2.5s, CLS under 0.1.
  • Enable CMS image optimization (auto-convert to WebP, auto-resize for breakpoints). This alone cuts 40–60% of image payload.
  • Enable asset minification under CMS → Settings → Performance. Minifies HTML, CSS, and JS at CDN level.
  • Set cache-control headers: HTML = no-cache, CSS/JS = 5 minutes, images = 1 year immutable.

Phase 4 — DNS Cutover (20 min)

  • Point DNS A record to the new host IP. TTL should already be lowered to 60 seconds (do this 24 hours before launch).
  • Confirm SSL certificate is provisioned and auto-renewing.
  • Wait for DNS propagation — check with dnschecker.org across multiple regions.
  • Test the live URL in an incognito window.

Phase 5 — Post-Launch Monitoring (10 min setup)

  • Submit sitemap to Google Search Console and Bing Webmaster Tools.
  • Set up uptime monitoring (UptimeRobot or similar) — alert to Slack if site returns non-200.
  • Verify GA4 is firing: check Realtime report while browsing the site.
  • Tag launch date in GA4 as an annotation for future comparison.
